ev_190.tag: Nuclear weapons reinforced regional stability and prevented major wars
ev_190.full_text: Another major conclusion of this study is that although nuclear weapons could have destabilizing consequences in certain situations, on net they have reinforced national security and regional stability in Asia. It is possible to argue that fledgling and small nuclear arsenals would be more vulnerable to preventive attacks; that the related strategic compulsion for early use may lead to early launch postures and crisis situations; that limited war under nuclear conditions to alter or restore the political status quo can intensify tensions and carry the risk of escalation to major war; that inadequate command, control, and safety measures could result in accidents; and that nuclear facilities and material may be vulnerable to terrorist attacks. These are legitimate concerns, but thus far nuclear weapons have not undermined national security and regional stability in Asia. Instead, they have ameliorated national security concerns, strengthened the status quo, increased deterrence dominance, prevented the outbreak of major wars, and reinforced the regional trend to reduce the salience of force in international politics. Nor have nuclear weapons had the predicted domino effect. These consequences have strengthened regional security and stability that rest on multiple pillars. The grim scenarios associated with nuclear weapons in Asia frequently rely on worst-case political and military situations; often they are seen in isolation from the national priorities of regional states that emphasize economic development and modernization through participation in regional and global economies and the high priority accorded to stability in domestic and international affairs. The primary goal of regional states is not aggrandizement through military aggression but preservation of national integrity, state or regime survival, economic growth and prosperity, increase in national power and international influence, preservation or incremental change in the status quo, and the construction of regional and global orders in which they are subjects rather than objects. Seen in this broader perspective, nuclear weapons and more generally military force are of greater relevance in the defense, deterrence, and assurance roles than offensive ones. This does not imply that offensive use of force or military clashes will not occur; only that force is not the first option, that military clashes will be infrequent, and that when they do occur they will be limited in scope and intensity. Security interaction in Asia increasingly approximates behavior associated with defensive realism.

ev_191.tag: Alliance credibility and relationship robustness prevent allied nuclear proliferation
ev_191.full_text: Recent academic research suggests that policymakers’ intuition about the efficacy of security assurances is correct; on average, nuclear security assurances decrease an ally’s likelihood of exploring, pursuing, and acquiring nuclear weapons.5 But, while policymakers and analysts appear to be correct that security assurances can prevent allied proliferation, much less is known about why some security assurances succeed and others fail. Many have put forth prescriptions meant to increase the effectiveness of U.S. security assurances, but little scholarship has rigorously theorized and tested these. 6 Without this knowledge, U.S. attempts to stem allied proliferation through security assurances may be suboptimal or, in the worst case, might even backfire. In addition to being policy relevant, the question of what makes security assurances effective in preventing allied proliferation also presents an interesting academic puzzle. In cases such as the United Kingdom immediately following World War II, South Korea, Sweden, and Japan, the United States provided security assurances to varying degrees of effect. In some cases, the assurances were very effective in preventing proliferation (i.e. Japan), whereas in others, they appear to have had less of an impact (i.e. South Korea in the 1970s).7 This puzzle – why assurances were more effective at preventing allied proliferation in some circumstances than others – has yet to be fully analyzed in the academic literature. To address this gap in our understanding, this article uses qualitative methods to help policymakers and academics better understand why assurances succeed – or fail – in preventing allied nuclear proliferation. Building on previous quantitative work showing that assurances can prevent such proliferation,8 this article takes this research a step further by asking why security assurances were more effective at preventing allied proliferation in some cases than others. To answer this question, we analyze two cases of allied proliferation: South Korea in the 1960s, 70s, and 80s and the United Kingdom immediately following World War II. By using a paired comparison of these cases, we explore when security assurances succeeded in preventing allied proliferation activity (in the 1980s in South Korea) and when they failed (in South Korea in the 1970s and in the United Kingdom after World War II). Our research suggests that certain factors do increase the likelihood that security assurances will prevent allied nuclear proliferation.9 The ally’s perception of the credibility of the patron’s assurance is extremely important; in both cases, South Korean and British perceptions of the credibility of the assurance – and the robustness of the overall relationship – played a major role in their proliferation decisions. The logic is straightforward: if the ally believes that the assurance is credible and that the patron is committed to the ally’s security, it will be less likely to engage in proliferation activity. As both South Korea and the United Kingdom feared that the United States was becoming less invested in their security, they increased their proliferation activity. Only when the ally began to perceive that the United States was firmly invested in its security did it curb its proliferation activity.

ev_192.tag: High credibility nuclear guarantees increase proliferation risk, not decrease it
ev_192.full_text: How does the alliance between a client state and its nuclear ally influence the probability that the client state chooses to proliferate? The literature on this question primarily looks at one mechanism through which nuclear allies have often tried to reassure client states about their security — nuclear security guarantees. These guarantees state that the nuclear ally is willing and prepared to use nuclear weapons to come to the client state’s aid if they face a severe security threat. A crucial characteristic of these guarantees is their credibility, or the degree to which it is believed that the nuclear ally will actually follow through on its promises. The commonly accepted theory linking nuclear security guarantees to the risk of proliferation is the “credibility hypothesis,” which posits that support for nuclear weapons acquisition is low when a nuclear security guarantee’s credibility is high and vice versa. However, my research offers a new theoretical approach. I posit that, under conditions of highly credible nuclear security guarantees, support for nuclear weapons acquisition should actually increase. This is because, as the credibility of a nuclear security guarantee increases, individuals become more concerned about unjustified or unfair use of nuclear weapons by their ally. This leads individuals in the client state support nuclear weapons acquisition in order to regain control over their nuclear fate. To adjudicate between these two theories, my project designs and analyzes a survey experiment among a representative sample of adults in South Korea in order to assess how the perceived credibility of a security guarantee from a nuclear-armed ally (in this case, the United States) influences public preferences about proliferation. Survey respondents were presented with a high-credibility security guarantee, a low-credibility security guarantee, or control condition and were then asked about their support for nuclear weapons acquisition. Ultimately, I find support for the unwanted use hypothesis, identifying a treatment effect whereby high-credibility nuclear security guarantees increase support for proliferation by between 2.60 and 4.87 percentage points.[1] ***BEGIN FOOTNOTE*** [1] These findings come from comparisons between the high-credibility treatment group and the control treatment group. ***END FOOTNOTE*** These findings challenge the consensus of the credibility hypothesis’ approach to nonproliferation strategies by nuclear-armed states. Ultimately, this project will provide a deeper understanding of alliance credibility in order to determine the conditions under which nuclear states can foster nonproliferation efforts in their allies. With the current turmoil on the Korean peninsula, an improved nonproliferation strategy could be vital to the U.S. ability to contain nuclear ambitions in South Korea and, ultimately, to promote the peaceful resolution of the ongoing nuclear crisis on the Korean Peninsula. Moreover, this research could be replicated and extended in other regions or with other types of alliances. For example, a revitalized investigation of the consequences of alliance credibility could have meaningful implications for the U.S. relationships with diverse non-nuclear allies, such as Germany, Japan, Taiwan, and Saudi Arabia. An increased U.S. capability to promote nonproliferation among its allies would offer a significant contribution to mitigating the risk of nuclear use worldwide.
ev_192.card: ... A crucial characteristic of these guarantees is their credibility, or the degree to which it is believed that the nuclear ally will actually follow through on its promises. The commonly accepted theory linking nuclear security guarantees to the risk of proliferation is the “credibility hypothesis,” which posits that support for nuclear weapons acquisition is low when a nuclear security guarantee’s credibility is high and vice versa. However, my research offers a new theoretical approach. **I posit that, under conditions of highly credible nuclear security guarantees, support for nuclear weapons acquisition should actually increase.** **This is because, as the credibility of a nuclear security guarantee increases, individuals become more concerned about unjustified or unfair use of nuclear weapons by their ally.** This leads individuals in the client state support nuclear weapons acquisition in order to regain control over their nuclear fate. To adjudicate between these two theories, my project designs and analyzes a survey experiment among a representative sample of adults in South Korea in order to assess how the perceived credibility of a security guarantee from a nuclear-armed ally (in this case, the United States) influences public preferences about proliferation. Survey respondents were presented with a high-credibility security guarantee, a low-credibility security guarantee, or control condition and were then asked about their support for nuclear weapons acquisition. ...
selected_texts: I posit that, under conditions of highly credible nuclear security guarantees, support for nuclear weapons acquisition should actually increase.
This is because, as the credibility of a nuclear security guarantee increases, individuals become more concerned about unjustified or unfair use of nuclear weapons by their ally.

ev_193.tag: Security assurances' success depends on perceived credibility and relationship robustness
ev_193.full_text: Recent academic research suggests that policymakers’ intuition about the efficacy of security assurances is correct; on average, nuclear security assurances decrease an ally’s likelihood of exploring, pursuing, and acquiring nuclear weapons.5 But, while policymakers and analysts appear to be correct that security assurances can prevent allied proliferation, much less is known about why some security assurances succeed and others fail. Many have put forth prescriptions meant to increase the effectiveness of U.S. security assurances, but little scholarship has rigorously theorized and tested these. 6 Without this knowledge, U.S. attempts to stem allied proliferation through security assurances may be suboptimal or, in the worst case, might even backfire. In addition to being policy relevant, the question of what makes security assurances effective in preventing allied proliferation also presents an interesting academic puzzle. In cases such as the United Kingdom immediately following World War II, South Korea, Sweden, and Japan, the United States provided security assurances to varying degrees of effect. In some cases, the assurances were very effective in preventing proliferation (i.e. Japan), whereas in others, they appear to have had less of an impact (i.e. South Korea in the 1970s).7 This puzzle – why assurances were more effective at preventing allied proliferation in some circumstances than others – has yet to be fully analyzed in the academic literature. To address this gap in our understanding, this article uses qualitative methods to help policymakers and academics better understand why assurances succeed – or fail – in preventing allied nuclear proliferation. Building on previous quantitative work showing that assurances can prevent such proliferation,8 this article takes this research a step further by asking why security assurances were more effective at preventing allied proliferation in some cases than others. To answer this question, we analyze two cases of allied proliferation: South Korea in the 1960s, 70s, and 80s and the United Kingdom immediately following World War II. By using a paired comparison of these cases, we explore when security assurances succeeded in preventing allied proliferation activity (in the 1980s in South Korea) and when they failed (in South Korea in the 1970s and in the United Kingdom after World War II). Our research suggests that certain factors do increase the likelihood that security assurances will prevent allied nuclear proliferation.9 The ally’s perception of the credibility of the patron’s assurance is extremely important; in both cases, South Korean and British perceptions of the credibility of the assurance – and the robustness of the overall relationship – played a major role in their proliferation decisions. The logic is straightforward: if the ally believes that the assurance is credible and that the patron is committed to the ally’s security, it will be less likely to engage in proliferation activity. As both South Korea and the United Kingdom feared that the United States was becoming less invested in their security, they increased their proliferation activity. Only when the ally began to perceive that the United States was firmly invested in its security did it curb its proliferation activity.

ev_194.tag: International NRRCs proven reliable mechanism for preventing nuclear disasters
ev_194.full_text: Despite growing foreign-policy disputes, Moscow and Washington have prevented an accidental nuclear disaster with extensive communication links through Nuclear Risk Reduction Centers (NRRCs), Russian Ambassador Yuri Ushakov said in a review of 20 years of the program established under President Reagan and Soviet leader Mikhail Gorbachev. "The NRRCs have proved their efficiency as an instrument of transparency and confidence-building," he said in a speech last week at the State Department. "Moreover, they have become a reliable mechanism designed to diminish and eliminate the very potential threat of an accidental nuclear disaster."
ev_194.card: Despite growing foreign-policy disputes, Moscow and Washington have prevented an accidental nuclear disaster with extensive communication links through Nuclear Risk Reduction Centers (NRRCs), Russian Ambassador Yuri Ushakov said in a review of 20 years of the program established under President Reagan and Soviet leader Mikhail Gorbachev. "The NRRCs have proved their efficiency as an instrument of transparency and confidence-building," he said in a speech last week at the State Department. **"Moreover, they have become a reliable mechanism designed to diminish and eliminate the very potential threat of an accidental nuclear disaster."**
selected_texts: "Moreover, they have become a reliable mechanism designed to diminish and eliminate the very potential threat of an accidental nuclear disaster."

ev_195.tag: Bilateral relationships necessary for sensitive intelligence sharing against non-state threats
ev_195.full_text: Relationships built on trust and mutual interests are also necessary to obtain cooperation from foreign governments in the area of Intelligence and Information. It is useful to separate the distinct, but related, issues of Intelligence Sharing and Intelligence Security Cooperation. Intelligence Sharing is a critical element in the fight against non-state threats, or strategic crime. By its nature, however, such sharing involves sensitive sources, methods and arrangements, normally in the context of a bilateral relationship. Its sensitivity requires delicate handling in highly restrictive channels. Intelligence sharing, in practice, falls outside the scope of Security Cooperation. Intelligence Security Cooperation, on the other hand, involves the development of interoperable and cooperative intelligence systems and processes designed to enhance the ability of one partner to work with one or several other partners. The core activities in Intelligence Security Cooperation are analytical and expertise exchanges, familiarization, training, and traditional Security Assistance. Both Intelligence Sharing and Intelligence Security Cooperation are mutually supporting. It is clear that the quality and reliability of intelligence we get from our partners depends on the competence, capability, professionalism, and trustworthiness of their national intelligence services and how compatible their operations are with ours. Intelligence Security Cooperation provides the tools to develop long-range relationships with foreign partners to improve both the quality of the intelligence we share and our ability to work together.

ev_196.tag: Five Eyes demonstrates effective bilateral intelligence network through cultural investment and trust
ev_196.full_text:
[Alasdair, "Suspicion Creeps Into the Five Eyes," Real Clear Defense, 9-3-2019, https://www.realcleardefense.com/articles/2019/09/03/suspicion_creeps_into_the_five_eyes_114715.html, accessed 9-4-20]
Intelligence sharing of this magnitude and length is possible only because of the “special relationship” – not just between the U.K. and the U.S., but also between Australia, Canada, and New Zealand. An enormous amount of cultural capital has been invested in the Five Eyes, and it has effectively become a self-sustaining network. The more these nations have cooperated, the more their trust and cooperation have deepened. Recent pressures may have tested their faith, but it’s a safe bet the Five Eyes will see this through.

ev_197.tag: Russia's intelligence cooperation essential for counterterrorism within multilateral framework
ev_197.full_text: That central point is that Russia matters a great deal to a U.S. government seeking to defend and advance its national interests. Prime Minister Vladimir Putin’s decision to return next year as president makes it all the more critical for Washington to manage its relationship with Russia through coherent, realistic policies. No one denies that Russia is a dangerous, difficult, often disappointing state to do business with. We should not overlook its many human rights and legal failures. Nonetheless, Russia is a player whose choices affect our vital interests in nuclear security and energy. It is key to supplying 100,000 U.S. troops fighting in Afghanistan and preventing Iran from acquiring nuclear weapons. Ten realities require U.S. policymakers to advance our nation’s interests by engaging and working with Moscow. First, Russia remains the only nation that can erase the United States from the map in 30 minutes. As every president since John F. Kennedy has recognized, Russia’s cooperation is critical to averting nuclear war. Second, Russia is our most consequential partner in preventing nuclear terrorism. Through a combination of more than $11 billion in U.S. aid, provided through the Nunn-Lugar Cooperative Threat Reduction program, and impressive Russian professionalism, two decades after the collapse of the “evil empire,” not one nuclear weapon has been found loose. Third, Russia plays an essential role in preventing the proliferation of nuclear weapons and missile-delivery systems. As Washington seeks to stop Iran’s drive toward nuclear weapons, Russian choices to sell or withhold sensitive technologies are the difference between failure and the possibility of success. Fourth, Russian support in sharing intelligence and cooperating in operations remains essential to the U.S. war to destroy Al Qaeda and combat other transnational terrorist groups. Fifth, Russia provides a vital supply line to 100,000 U.S. troops fighting in Afghanistan. As U.S. relations with Pakistan have deteriorated, the Russian lifeline has grown ever more important and now accounts for half all daily deliveries. Sixth, Russia is the world’s largest oil producer and second largest gas producer. Over the past decade, Russia has added more oil and gas exports to world energy markets than any other nation. Most major energy transport routes from Eurasia start in Russia or cross its nine time zones. As citizens of a country that imports two of every three of the 20 million barrels of oil that fuel U.S. cars daily, Americans feel Russia’s impact at our gas pumps. Seventh, Moscow is an important player in today’s international system. It is no accident that Russia is one of the five veto-wielding, permanent members of the U.N. Security Council, as well as a member of the G-8 and G-20. A Moscow more closely aligned with U.S. goals would be significant in the balance of power to shape an environment in which China can emerge as a global power without overturning the existing order. Eighth, Russia is the largest country on Earth by land area, abutting China on the East, Poland in the West and the United States across the Arctic. This territory provides transit corridors for supplies to global markets whose stability is vital to the U.S. economy. Ninth, Russia’s brainpower is reflected in the fact that it has won more Nobel Prizes for science than all of Asia, places first in most math competitions and dominates the world chess masters list. The only way U.S. astronauts can now travel to and from the International Space Station is to hitch a ride on Russian rockets. The co-founder of the most advanced digital company in the world, Google, is Russian-born Sergei Brin. Tenth, Russia’s potential as a spoiler is difficult to exaggerate. Consider what a Russian president intent on frustrating U.S. international objectives could do — from stopping the supply flow to Afghanistan to selling S-300 air defense missiles to Tehran to joining China in preventing U.N. Security Council resolutions. So next time you hear a policymaker dismissing Russia with rhetoric about “who cares?” ask them to identify nations that matter more to U.S. success, or failure, in advancing our national interests.

ev_198.tag: NPT framework created legal barriers preventing horizontal proliferation
ev_198.full_text: 2: The Future of the Nuclear Nonproliferation Regime Even as their commitment to bilateral arms control wavers, the United States and Russia remain pillars of the global nonproliferation regime. While Washington and Moscow continue to pursue a largely collaborative approach to nonproliferation, the future of that collaboration looks increasingly questionable for reasons having to do with internal developments (especially in the United States) and larger global shifts. The international community has done a decent job of preventing horizontal nuclear proliferation. This relative success has resulted from the presence of legal, political, and normative barriers in the Non- Proliferation Treaty (NPT) framework; the United States’ ability to make allies feel secure enough not to pursue nuclear capabilities; and the sense that clandestine nuclear development will be caught—largely because the United States and Russia have historically been united in opposing nuclear weapons programs of both friendly and hostile regimes. That shared commitment, however, appears increasingly open to question. Participants identified several reasons why continued U.S.-Russian collaboration around nonproliferation appears uncertain. These include Washington’s mounting estrangement from the international system and multilateral agreements—a phenomenon that the Trump administration has accelerated but not created. Views of the international order have become bogged down in partisan politics in the United States, which makes it more difficult for the United States to adopt positions enjoying bipartisan consensus—and for other states to trust that U.S. commitments (such as the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action with Iran) will endure across administrations. Increased U.S. insularity could also weaken the effectiveness of U.S. sanctions and other coercive measures aimed at countering proliferation, especially as steps by Russia and others to bypass the U.S.-dominated global financial architecture continue. Growing U.S.-Russia (and U.S.-China) competition could itself jeopardize cooperation on nonproliferation as well. The danger of nonproliferation taking a backseat to other policy objectives (as has already been the case with regard to India and Pakistan) could grow as Washington and Moscow come to focus more on great power competition. Increasing competition also makes it more difficult for the United States and Russia to negotiate new agreements related to arms control and nonproliferation at a time when more states are challenging the strictures of the NPT. Many states, including rising “middle powers,” believe the United States and Russia have not lived up to their NPT commitments to reduce their own nuclear arsenals. Perception that the current system is unjust will push more of these middle powers to develop nuclear programs.

ev_200.tag: International agreements form critical components of nonproliferation regime
ev_200.full_text: Areas for Future U.S.-Russia Science Diplomacy Recent progress in the U.S.-Russia relationship provides fertile ground for science diplomacy between the two nations, as do the areas where there has not been progress. As Assistant Secretary Gottemoeller noted, the U.S.-Russian Agreement for Cooperation in the Field of Peaceful Uses of Nuclear Energy, also known as the U.S.-Russia 123 Agreement, came into force on January 11, 2011. The agreement normalizes and expands cooperation in nuclear energy and enables cooperation on technology development for nuclear nonproliferation programs, nuclear forensics, and safeguards and monitoring programs. The 123 Agreement is only a legal framework authorizing such work, so it is now up to the parties to identify the substantive work to be done under the agreement and to take the steps necessary to make that joint work successful. Secretary Perry listed a set of technical issues the world will face as it moves toward a follow-on arms control treaty and lower numbers of nuclear weapons, or zero nuclear weapons. They range from cooperation on ballistic missile defense to verification of warheads and treaties. Secretary Perry called these technical challenges special challenges to CISAC and others in the technical community engaged in science diplomacy. His list, as well as issues highlighted by Dr. Garwin and others, are as follows: Safeguarding Nuclear Power and Contributing to Nonproliferation Regimes “When we are considering the export of nuclear reactors and fuel to third countries, we need to make sure that we arrive at a solution that will not contribute to proliferation.” —Nikolai N. Ponomarev-Stepnoy Nuclear power is inextricably linked to a risk of proliferation because of the materials and technologies involved. Some nuclear-fuel-cycle technologies and some nuclear materials are less attractive or less effective for nuclear-weapons applications, but some level of risk always remains. For this reason, a variety of policy measures and physical safeguards has been put in place, and numerous others are being analyzed and considered. International agreements and commitments to physical safeguards, technologies, efforts for detection of undeclared facilities, and export controls for sensitive technologies, all contribute to a healthy nuclear nonproliferation regime. Because of the technical complexities of nearly every aspect of the nuclear fuel cycle and its potential exploitation for proliferation, science diplomacy can continue to make substantial contributions on this topic. Verifying Nuclear Arms Reductions “These negotiations [over reductions in strategic and nonstrategic arsenals] will only be fruitful or productive if the parties are successful in establishing mutually acceptable verification mechanisms.” —Viktor I. Yesin “Establishing the verification measures in transparency tools . . . will help us ensure confidence as we move from step to step. This isn’t going to happen without a strong scientific and technology base, because we have to have confidence that we collectively, the big we, know what’s going on in this area.” —Under Secretary of Energy Thomas D’Agostino In the debate over advice and consent on the ratification of the New START, the U.S. Senate made clear that the next step for nuclear arms control must include nonstrategic (tactical) nuclear weapons in Russia. Assistant Secretary Gottemoeller noted that the United States and Russia are preparing for a dialogue on nonstrategic nuclear weapons, and she highlighted the role of NGOs in providing analysis and ideas for this dialogue, “providing much, very welcome food for thought for those of us working these matters inside the government.” Under Secretary D’Agostino noted that a prominent challenge among the many associated with including tactical nuclear weapons in an arms control or reduction treaty is verification. Verification is also perhaps the topic most susceptible to technical options. General Viktor I. Yesin, former chief of staff of the Strategic Rocket Forces in Russia, proposed a course of action for verification of reductions in tactical nuclear weapons: (1) declare the number of existing weapons, (2) categorize the weapons into an active stockpile (deployable) and an inactive reserve subject to elimination, (3) agree that inactive reserve weapons cannot be made active, and (4) separate the storage facilities for the two categories. If these conditions are met, there could be on-site inspections of storage sites of both categories of weapons. The goal of each on-site inspection would be to ensure that the number of warheads in each storage facility does not exceed what is declared. The number in each category sent to elimination is also verifiable based on the amount of nuclear material obtained from elimination. There are technical details in General Yesin’s ideas that would require further development and refinement, as lingering doubts about possible undeclared sites and shielded weapons, among other issues, would need to be addressed. The same is true for nearly any such proposal. Secretary Perry said that the verification of warheads, as distinct from missiles and deployments, is a great technological challenge. Joint exploration and development of technical options to enable proposals for verification of declarations and reductions is a valuable topic where science diplomacy has an essential role to play. Countering Nuclear Terrorism “Should we really wait for nuclear terrorism, compared to which 9/11 will appear an innocent joke?” —Evgeniy Avrorin “[T]he stocks [of highly enriched uranium (HEU) and plutonium] have grown. Terrorism has become a serious problem. But the sense of danger seems to be muted, except perhaps for some people here today.” —John Ahearne It has been noted that the knowledge of how to build a crude nuclear explosive is within the reach of many, and that the difficulty in acquiring the fissile material for the nuclear explosive is the main obstacle to nuclear terrorism. Little progress has been made on the Fissile Material Cutoff Treaty (FMCT), and even this measure would only stop future production of fissile material, not address nuclear material already in existence. In discussing disposition of HEU and plutonium, Ahearne expressed his view that the sheer quantity of HEU and plutonium in storage is a hazard. Some aspects of verification of an FMCT and declarations of existing stocks are difficult, but for those who share Dr. Ahearne’s concern about stocks, the challenges cannot be avoided. D’Agostino and Gottemoeller both highlighted additional joint activities, such as nuclear forensics, which could work to curb nuclear terrorism.

ev_201.tag: Systemic conflict decreases as number of nuclear states increases
ev_201.full_text: The main findings reveal that the optimist expectation of the relationship between nuclear proliferation and interstate conflict is empirically supported:9 first, a larger number of nuclear states on average decreases the systemic propensity for interstate conflict; and second, there is no clear evidence that the emergence of new nuclear states increases the systemic propensity for interstate conflict. Gartzke and Jo (2009) argue that nuclear weapons themselves have no exogenous effect on the probability of conflict, because when a state is engaged in or expects to engage in conflict, it may develop nuclear weapons to keep fighting, or to prepare for, that conflict. If this selection effect existed, the analysis should overestimate the conflict-provoking effect of nuclear proliferation in the above model. Still, the results indicate that a larger number of nuclear states are associated with fewer disputes in the system. This conclusion, however, raises questions about how to reconcile this study’s findings with those of a recent quantitative dyadic-level study (Bell and Miller, 2015). The current paper finds that nuclear proliferation decreases the systemic propensity for interstate conflict, while Bell and Miller (2015) find that nuclear symmetry has no significant effect on dyadic conflict, but that nuclear asymmetry is associated with a higher probability of dyadic conflict. It is possible that nuclear proliferation decreases conflict through the conflict-mitigating effects of extended nuclear deterrence and/or fear of nuclear states’ intervention, to the extent that these effects overwhelm the conflict-provoking effect of nuclear–asymmetrical dyads. Thus, dyadic-level empirics cannot solely be relied on to infer causal links between nuclear proliferation and a systemic propensity for conflict. The systemic-level empirics deserve attention.

ev_202.tag: Nuclear proliferation may be welcomed for systemic conflict reduction
ev_202.full_text: The main findings reveal that the optimist expectation of the relationship between nuclear proliferation and interstate conflict is empirically supported:9 first, a larger number of nuclear states on average decreases the systemic propensity for interstate conflict; and second, there is no clear evidence that the emergence of new nuclear states increases the systemic propensity for interstate conflict. Gartzke and Jo (2009) argue that nuclear weapons themselves have no exogenous effect on the probability of conflict, because when a state is engaged in or expects to engage in conflict, it may develop nuclear weapons to keep fighting, or to prepare for, that conflict. If this selection effect existed, the analysis should overestimate the conflict-provoking effect of nuclear proliferation in the above model. Still, the results indicate that a larger number of nuclear states are associated with fewer disputes in the system. This conclusion, however, raises questions about how to reconcile this study’s findings with those of a recent quantitative dyadic-level study (Bell and Miller, 2015). The current paper finds that nuclear proliferation decreases the systemic propensity for interstate conflict, while Bell and Miller (2015) find that nuclear symmetry has no significant effect on dyadic conflict, but that nuclear asymmetry is associated with a higher probability of dyadic conflict. It is possible that nuclear proliferation decreases conflict through the conflict-mitigating effects of extended nuclear deterrence and/or fear of nuclear states’ intervention, to the extent that these effects overwhelm the conflict-provoking effect of nuclear–asymmetrical dyads. Thus, dyadic-level empirics cannot solely be relied on to infer causal links between nuclear proliferation and a systemic propensity for conflict. The systemic-level empirics deserve attention. The findings of this paper also have significant policy implications. The international community is sensitive to nuclear proliferation, and with Iran well on the way to developing full nuclear capabilities, it is crucial to understand the implications of nuclear proliferation for international security. This paper suggests that, at least in terms of a systemic propensity for interstate conflict, nuclear proliferation might be welcomed – although, given that nuclear asymmetry can provoke dyadic conflict, reducing this side effect of nuclear proliferation by some other measure will be desirable. Finally, this paper should not be seen as decisive evidence that nuclear proliferation contributes to international security in general. Nuclear proliferation may increase risks of nuclear weapons being leaked to terrorist groups (Bueno de Mesquita and Riker, 1982: 304) or used accidentally (Intriligator and Brito, 1981). It is untenable to assess the merits and demerits of nuclear proliferation only in terms of a systemic propensity for conflict. Additional research should examine these risks. Nonetheless, this paper makes a significant contribution to the literature by adding new empirics for a more comprehensive assessment of the relationship between nuclear proliferation and interstate conflict.

ev_204.tag: Systemic conflict propensity decreases with more nuclear states (empirical finding)
ev_204.cite: Suzuki, Institute for international conflict resolution and reconstruction, 15
ev_204.fullcite: Suzuki, Institute for international conflict resolution and reconstruction, 15
ev_204.full_text: In the literature on nuclear proliferation, some argue that further proliferation decreases interstate conflict, some say that it increases interstate conflict, and others indicate a non-linear relationship between these two factors. However, there has been no systematic empirical investigation on the relationship between nuclear proliferation and a propensity for conflict at the interstate–systemic level. To fill this gap, the current paper uses the machine learning method Random Forests, which can investigate complex non-linear relationships between dependent and independent variables, and which can identify important regressors from a group of all potential regressors in explaining the relationship between nuclear proliferation and the propensity for conflict. The results indicate that, on average, a larger number of nuclear states decrease the systemic propensity for interstate conflict, while the emergence of new nuclear states does not have an important effect. This paper also notes, however, that scholars should investigate other risks of proliferation to assess whether nuclear proliferation is better or worse for international peace and security in general. Does nuclear proliferation decrease or increase interstate conflict? The existing theories provide different, and often conflicting, explanations. Quantitative research has assessed the empirical validity of various explanations, but has only utilized a dyadic-level or crisis-level of analysis (Asal and Beardsley, 2007; Bell and Miller, 2015; Gartzke and Jo, 2009; Geller, 1990; Narang, 2013; Rauchhaus, 2009; Sobek et al., 2012). This is surprising, because nuclear proliferation is principally a systemic phenomenon, since it increases the number of nuclear states in the interstate system. Thus, it remains unclear how nuclear proliferation is empirically associated with a propensity for interstate conflict at the interstate–systemic level. A systemic propensity for conflict may not be understood simply as an aggregate of lower levels of phenomena. For example, even if nuclear weapons had a significant effect on conflict at the dyadic level, this effect might be negligible as an aggregate at the systemic level. This is a reasonable concern, because the majority of states in the system are non-nuclear states. Given these issues, this paper contributes to the existing literature by empirically examining how a change in the number of nuclear states influences a propensity for conflict at the interstate–systemic level. Because the literature lacks a rigorous empirical answer to the theoretical debate, like Rauchhaus (2009) this paper tests existing theories rather than proposing a new theory. To this end, it utilizes the machine learning method Random Forests (Breiman, 2001; for an R package, see Liaw and Wiener, 2002), one of the best-performing supervised learning models currently available (Caruana and Niculescu-Mizil, 2006). Random Forests can address theoretical and methodological problems to analyze the relationship between nuclear proliferation and a systemic propensity for interstate conflict, as this paper discusses in greater detail. The paper first reviews the scholarly debate on nuclear proliferation and interstate conflict. Second, it empirically examines the relationship between nuclear proliferation and a systemic propensity for interstate conflict, using a Random Forests model to analyze interstate–systemic year data from 1950 to2009. Finally, it presents implications for the literature and policy making.

Does nuclear proliferation decrease or increase interstate conflict? The existing theories provide different, and often conflicting, explanations. ...

### VALUE 2: Collective security through international cooperation surpasses unilateral military buildup


---
INSTRUCTIONS:

2. Use the SUB-BELIEFS to structure your warrants
3. Cite the EVIDENCE using the provided cards
4. Reference belief IDs (e.g., belief_1) when building your skeleton

speeches.AC:
Judge, my opponent will tell you that more nuclear weapons mean more risk, more accidents, more war. I am going to prove the opposite. Nuclear proliferation increases international stability because it expands deterrence relationships through mutual assured destruction. This is not theoretical speculation. This is how the world avoided nuclear war for seventy-five years. This is why no major power has attacked another with conventional forces since nineteen forty-five. Let me explain why.

Contest one: Nuclear proliferation expands deterrence relationships. Every new nuclear state immediately creates at least one new deterrence relationship with each existing nuclear power. This is the foundation of stability.

Here’s why this matters. When a country develops nuclear weapons, it doesn’t just gain a weapon. It gains psychological immunity. Existing nuclear powers must now account for that new capability. They must consider the consequences of aggression. This isn’t speculation. This is the core mechanism through which proliferation increases stability.

The NEG will likely argue that new nuclear states are less responsible, more prone to miscalculation. But that’s not how deterrence works. Deterrence isn’t about perfect leadership. It’s about the certainty of catastrophic retaliation. As Reisman writes in twenty eighteen, this system of reciprocal defense must assume a world of equals. In other words, mutual vulnerability creates mutual caution. Every new nuclear actor forces all major powers to operate under that assumption.

Contest two: A larger number of nuclear states is empirically associated with fewer disputes. This is the counterintuitive finding that proves the resolution.

Suzukiâ€™s twenty fifteen quantitative analysis demonstrates this directly. His research shows a larger number of nuclear states are associated with fewer disputes in the international system. This isn’t a minor correlation. This is a systemic pattern. More nuclear powers, less conflict.

Why does this happen? Because deterrence is not zero-sum. It is networked. When Japan relies on the United States nuclear umbrella, when India and Pakistan both possess nuclear weapons, when North Korea has developed its program, each relationship creates additional barriers to aggression. The potential costs of war multiply with each new nuclear actor.

Contest three: Let me explain the mechanism. Mutual Assured Destruction creates a stable equilibrium where no rational leader would initiate a nuclear conflict because it guarantees catastrophic retaliation.

This is not a theory about perfect peace. It is a theory about catastrophic risk. The logic is simple: if Country A attacks Country B, Country B can destroy Country A. Therefore, Country A has no incentive to attack. The fear of annihilation prevents aggression because the costs outweigh any potential gains.

This is why the Cold War did not escalate to nuclear exchange. Why no major power has invaded a NATO member. The system works because leaders understand that nuclear war has no winners.

Contest four: The NEG will raise the objection that more nuclear weapons increase the risk of accidents, miscalculation, or unauthorized use. I accept this risk exists. But this is not a reason to reject the resolution. It is a reason to manage that risk.

The AFF case is about systemic stability, not risk elimination. Even if accidents occur, the overall stability created by deterrence relationships remains intact. The question is not whether nuclear weapons are perfect. The question is whether they increase or decrease the likelihood of large-scale aggression between major powers.

In conclusion, the resolution is simple: nuclear proliferation increases international stability. It does so by expanding deterrence relationships, creating interconnected security networks that discourage conflict, and establishing the mutual assured destruction dynamic that has prevented nuclear war for generations. The NEGâ€™s case rests on hypothetical accidents. The AFF case rests on empirical evidence and proven theory. More nuclear states, less conflict. That is the pattern. That is the stability we must recognize. I urge an affirmative ballot.
speeches.AC-CX:
Q1 (NEG): Your Suzuki 2015 evidence claims that more nuclear states lead to fewer disputes, yet the India-Pakistan rivalry has seen multiple major disputes since nuclearization—including the 2019 Pulwama attack and Balakot strike—where conventional conflict was narrowly averted. How does Suzuki 2015 account for these disputes as part of a "fewer disputes" pattern?

A1 (AFF): Thank you for your question. The 2019 Pulwama attack and Balakot strike are not a counterexample—they are a *textbook illustration* of how nuclear proliferation increases stability. Suzuki 2015’s evidence (ev_62) shows that *a larger number of nuclear states are associated with fewer disputes in the system*—meaning the *overall pattern* of conflict decreases as nuclear states proliferate. The 2019 incident was a conventional clash that *remained conventional* precisely because both India and Pakistan possess nuclear weapons. This is the deterrence mechanism Suzuki documented: nuclear weapons prevent escalation to catastrophic conflict. 

Moreover, Cohen 2017 (ev_67) confirms that *experience with nuclear weapons moderates conflict propensity*, meaning disputes like this become less frequent and severe over time. The 2019 incident actually *supports* Suzuki’s finding—it’s a case where deterrence worked to stop escalation, not a failure of the system. Suzuki’s study examines the *entire historical pattern*, not isolated events. When we look at the data, nuclear proliferation correlates with *fewer disputes overall*—not more. The resolution holds because nuclear weapons create the stability that prevents conflicts from spiraling. This isn’t speculation; it’s the pattern Suzuki’s evidence proves.

Q2 (NEG): How does Suzuki 2015's evidence account for the fact that the 2019 India-Pakistan military standoff—a major dispute that occurred *after* both states were nuclear-armed and that the Pakistan government described in PTI 2019 as requiring nuclear deterrence to de-escalate—fits into the "fewer disputes" pattern?

A2 (AFF): The 2019 India-Pakistan standoff is *not* a counterexample to Suzuki 2015 – it's a textbook validation of the study. Suzuki's evidence (ev_62) shows a *larger number of nuclear states correlates with fewer disputes in the system* – meaning fewer disputes escalate to catastrophic levels. The 2019 incident was a major dispute *that remained conventional* because both sides were nuclear-armed, as Pakistan explicitly stated in PTI 2019: "nuclear deterrence was required to de-escalate." That's exactly the deterrence mechanism Suzuki documented. Suzuki doesn't claim disputes vanish; it claims nuclear proliferation *reduces the severity and frequency of escalations*. The 2019 event fits the pattern perfectly: it was contained by deterrence, not a failure of it. Suzuki's study examines the *entire historical pattern* – and when we look at the data, nuclear proliferation correlates with *fewer* major disputes, not more. The 2019 incident supports Suzuki, it doesn't contradict it. This is why the resolution holds: more nuclear states create the interconnected deterrence networks that prevent conflicts from spiraling.

Q3 (NEG): PTI 2019 states that Pakistan described the 2019 incident as requiring nuclear deterrence to de-escalate. If nuclear proliferation increases stability, why did the region need nuclear deterrence to prevent a conventional conflict from escalating? Doesn't this indicate that proliferation has increased the risk of crisis, not decreased it?

A3 (AFF): The PTI 2019 statement that Pakistan "required nuclear deterrence to de-escalate" is not evidence of instability—it's *proof of stability*. Suzuki 2015 (ev_62) demonstrates that a larger number of nuclear states correlates with *fewer disputes in the system* precisely because nuclear deterrence *prevents escalation* like the 2019 incident from becoming war. Pakistan needed nuclear deterrence to de-escalate *because* it worked: the conflict remained conventional, as Suzuki's data predicts. Without nuclear weapons, the 2019 clash would likely have escalated to nuclear exchange—proving proliferation *reduced* catastrophic risk. The NEG's argument confuses conventional crises (which still happen) with the *absence of war* (which proliferation ensures). Suzuki's evidence shows proliferation *decreases* the severity and frequency of disputes, and the 2019 incident is a perfect example of that pattern. It's not a counterexample—it's the mechanism in action.

Q4 (NEG): Your Suzuki 2015 evidence claims nuclear proliferation leads to fewer disputes. But since 1998, when India and Pakistan both became nuclear-armed, they've had multiple major disputes: the 2001 Parliament attack, 2008 Mumbai attacks, and 2019 Pulwama/Balakot crisis. How does Suzuki 2015 explain the *increase* in major disputes between these two states after nuclearization, which directly contradicts the 'fewer disputes' pattern?

A4 (AFF): You're absolutely right that India and Pakistan have had major disputes since 1998—including the 2019 incident. But that's precisely why Suzuki 2015's evidence (ev_62) *supports* our resolution, not contradicts it. Suzuki measured *systemic disputes*—not single dyads—and found that as nuclear states proliferate, the *overall number of disputes in the international system decreases*. Why? Because nuclear deterrence prevents these disputes from escalating to war. The 2019 incident is a textbook example: Pakistan explicitly stated nuclear deterrence was required to de-escalate (PTI 2019), meaning the conflict *remained conventional*—exactly as Suzuki predicted. Without nuclear weapons, this would have likely become a full-scale war. 

Moreover, Cohen 2017 (ev_67) proves this pattern: "experience with nuclear weapons moderates conflict propensity." India-Pakistan's disputes are *less frequent and severe* than pre-nuclear rivalries (e.g., the 1965 war), and they're trending downward. Suzuki's data shows that *more nuclear states correlate with fewer disputes systemically*—not that disputes vanish. The 2019 incident isn't a counterexample; it's evidence of the mechanism in action. That's why the resolution holds: proliferation increases stability by making *all* disputes less dangerous, not by eliminating them.
speeches.NC:
Judge, my opponent claims nuclear proliferation increases stability. I will prove the opposite: nuclear proliferation actively destroys stability by making arms control impossible and triggering regional crises. Let me explain why.

First, arms control complexity grows exponentially with every new nuclear state. As Johnson twenty twenty-two states: "Policy implications How can decision-makers mitigate the inadvertent escalation risks associated with AI and nuclear sys..." This evidence proves proliferation doesn't create stability—it creates a nightmare of coordination. When we add nuclear states, we multiply communication channels, verification challenges, and crisis points. The Nuclear Nonproliferation Treaty was designed for a handful of powers, not the fragmented reality we face today. Johnson twenty twenty-two shows decision-makers are already struggling to manage these risks. The more nuclear states there are, the harder it becomes to prevent accidents or miscalculations. This isn't theory—it's why arms control frameworks like the NPT are collapsing. Reaching Critical Will estimates only five of twenty-two disarmament actions agreed in two thousand ten were implemented by two thousand fifteen. That's not stability—that's systemic failure. The AFF's Suzuki evidence ignores this reality, pretending treaties can handle exponential complexity. But Johnson twenty twenty-two proves they can't.

Second, nuclear proliferation triggers regional arms races that create constant instability, not peace. Look at India and Pakistan. After both became nuclear-armed, they had multiple crises including the two thousand nineteen Pulwama attack and Balakot strike. The Pakistani government itself admitted nuclear deterrence was required to de-escalate: "Islamabad: Nuclear deterrence led to the de-escalation of tensions during the post-Pulwama military stand-off between In..." This isn't proof of stability—it's proof of crisis. If proliferation truly increased stability, why would Pakistan need to invoke nuclear deterrence to prevent a conventional conflict from escalating? The very fact that they had to do this shows the region is unstable. Nuclear weapons didn't prevent the crisis—they only stopped it from becoming nuclear. The AFF calls this "stability," but it's an "ugly stability" where every crisis requires nuclear brinkmanship. As the perspective_node states, this is evidence that proliferation "has not enhanced regional stability."

The AFF claims Suzuki two thousand fifteen shows "fewer disputes" with more nuclear states. But Suzuki's study counts disputes as "fewer" only when they remain conventional. It ignores how proliferation *creates* those disputes in the first place. The India-Pakistan case is textbook: nuclearization didn't reduce disputes—it made them more frequent. Before nuclear weapons, India and Pakistan had major wars like in nineteen sixty-five. After, they had the Parliament attack, Mumbai attacks, and Pulwama—all while needing nuclear deterrence to avoid escalation. Suzuki's data misses this because it only measures outcomes, not the constant pressure of new crises. The AFF's evidence is self-serving propaganda from Pakistani officials promoting the narrative of stability. It doesn't reflect reality.

The judge knows that stability means no crises at all, not just crises that don't escalate to war. When Pakistan describes needing nuclear deterrence to de-escalate, they're admitting the region is unstable. If proliferation increased stability, they wouldn't need that safety net. The AFF's case is built on a false premise: that avoiding nuclear war equals stability. But stability means avoiding *all* conflict—conventional and nuclear. Nuclear proliferation does the opposite. It makes the world more fragile, not less.

In conclusion, the resolution is false. Nuclear proliferation increases arms control complexity to unmanageable levels and triggers regional arms races that create constant crises. Johnson twenty twenty-two proves the first point—arms control is collapsing under proliferation's weight. PTI twenty nineteen proves the second—nuclear deterrence is a tool for managing instability, not creating it. The AFF's Suzuki evidence is a statistical mirage that ignores the real-world cost of proliferation. More nuclear states mean more crises requiring nuclear deterrence, not fewer disputes. This isn't stability—it's a house of cards waiting to fall. I urge a negative ballot.
speeches.NC-CX:
Q1 (AFF): You cited Pakistan's admission that nuclear deterrence "led to the de-escalation of tensions" during the Pulwama/Balakot crisis - doesn't this actually prove that nuclear weapons prevented conventional war from escalating, which is the definition of stability?

A1 (NEG): You're absolutely right that Pakistan's admission states nuclear deterrence 'led to de-escalation' - that's exactly what I cited. But this proves the opposite of what you're claiming. Let me explain why with clear logic:

If nuclear proliferation truly increased stability, why would Pakistan need to invoke nuclear deterrence as a safety net during a conventional crisis? That's the critical point. The fact that they had to say "nuclear deterrence prevented escalation" proves the region was unstable to begin with.

Think of it this way: if your neighborhood had true stability, you wouldn't need to keep a gun in your nightstand. You'd only need it if you're constantly worried about break-ins. Nuclear deterrence is like that gun in the nightstand - it's a reactive safety measure, not proof of peace.

The AFF's entire argument relies on conflating "no nuclear war" with "stability." But stability means no crises at all, not just crises that don't escalate to nuclear weapons. When Pakistan describes needing nuclear deterrence to de-escalate, they're admitting the region is unstable. That's why I called it "ugly stability" - the kind that requires constant nuclear brinkmanship to prevent conventional conflict from spiraling.

This isn't a concession against me - it's proof that proliferation creates a world where every crisis requires nuclear deterrence as a safety net. That's not stability. That's fragility.

Q2 (AFF): You just said Pakistan needed nuclear deterrence to prevent conventional conflict from escalating - doesn't that mean nuclear weapons successfully prevented a larger war?

A2 (NEG): Let me be direct: yes, nuclear weapons prevented a nuclear exchange in that specific crisis. But that's not what stability means. Let me explain why this actually proves my case.

Think about what Pakistan's admission really says: "Nuclear deterrence led to the de-escalation." The key word is "led to." They're saying the only reason the crisis didn't escalate to nuclear war was because of nuclear weapons. That's not proof of stability - that's proof of a world where nuclear weapons are required to prevent conventional conflicts from spiraling out of control.

Imagine if your neighbor told you, "I'm glad I have a gun because it prevented my dog from getting into your yard." That doesn't prove your neighborhood is safe. It proves your neighborhood is so unsafe that you need a gun to prevent a dog from getting into someone else's yard.

That's exactly what's happening with nuclear proliferation. When Pakistan says nuclear deterrence prevented escalation, they're admitting the region is unstable enough that conventional crises require nuclear brinkmanship. That's not stability - that's fragility. Every crisis now requires nuclear deterrence as a safety net. That's the definition of an unstable system.

The AFF wants you to believe that avoiding nuclear war equals stability. But stability means no crises at all, not just crises that don't escalate to nuclear weapons. When nuclear weapons become the primary tool for managing regional conflicts, that's not stability - that's a house of cards waiting to fall. More nuclear states mean more crises requiring this safety net. That's why my argument stands: nuclear proliferation increases international instability, not the other way around.

Q3 (AFF): You just said nuclear deterrence prevented escalation in Pulwama/Balakot - so you're agreeing that nuclear weapons worked to prevent a larger war, right?

A3 (NEG): Let me be clear: yes, nuclear weapons prevented a nuclear exchange in that specific crisis. But that's precisely why this proves my case, not yours. Let me give you a simple analogy the judge can understand.

Imagine your neighbor has a gun. One day, a fight breaks out between your kids and your neighbor's kids. The fight gets heated, but your neighbor doesn't shoot - he just says, "I have a gun, so I'm not going to shoot." Did the gun create stability? No. The gun created a situation where every dispute required a gun to prevent escalation.

That's exactly what's happening with nuclear proliferation. When Pakistan says nuclear deterrence "led to de-escalation," they're admitting the region is unstable enough that conventional crises require nuclear brinkmanship to prevent escalation. The fact that they need to invoke nuclear weapons as a safety net proves the system is fragile, not stable.

Stability isn't about preventing the worst-case scenario. Stability is about not needing to rely on the worst-case scenario to prevent conflict in the first place. The Pulwama/Balakot crisis proves nuclear proliferation creates a world where every conventional dispute requires nuclear deterrence as a safety net. That's not stability - that's a house of cards waiting to fall. More nuclear states mean more crises requiring this safety net. That's why the resolution is false.

Q4 (AFF): You cited Johnson 2022 as proving proliferation "destroys arms control stability" - can you quote the exact passage from Johnson 2022 that discusses nuclear proliferation specifically?

A4 (NEG): You're absolutely right to ask for the exact quote - and I'll be transparent: in my speech, I referenced Johnson 2022 generally rather than quoting the specific passage about nuclear proliferation. That was a weakness in my evidence presentation.

But here's why this doesn't undermine my argument: the Johnson 2022 evidence I cited is about decision-makers struggling to "mitigate inadvertent escalation risks associated with AI and nuclear systems." The key point isn't the exact wording about nuclear proliferation - it's the broader implication that as nuclear complexity increases, decision-makers face exponentially harder challenges.

Think of it this way: if arms control was simple with multiple nuclear states, we wouldn't be having these coordination problems. The fact that decision-makers are already struggling with escalation risks proves my point - proliferation makes arms control more complex, not less. The evidence supports the conclusion even if I didn't provide the exact line about proliferation.

This isn't about misrepresenting evidence - it's about the logical connection between proliferation, complexity, and the real-world challenges decision-makers face. The Johnson 2022 evidence stands on that broader point.
speeches.1AR:
Thank you. Let me crystallize this debate into one clear question: does preventing escalation equal stability? The answer is yes, and my opponent's own evidence proves it.

Voting issue one: The Pulwama Balakot incident is not a counterexample to the resolution. It is the resolution in action. Pakistan's government admitted nuclear deterrence was required to de-escalate. That is not evidence of instability. That is evidence of stability. It means a conventional conflict remained conventional because both sides understood the catastrophic consequences of escalation. The only reason this crisis did not become war is because nuclear weapons created a rational incentive for restraint. My opponent says this proves the region is unstable. I say it proves the deterrence mechanism works. When my opponent concedes nuclear weapons prevented escalation, they concede the resolution is true.

Voting issue two: The NEG's arms control complexity argument misses the point entirely. Johnson twenty twenty-two discusses decision-makers struggling with escalation risks. But struggling to manage risk is not the same as proving proliferation destroys stability. The NEG never explains why more nuclear states should be less stable rather than more stable. They never explain why the Cold War, with its two nuclear superpowers, was less stable than a world with more nuclear powers. They have no evidence. They have no theory. They have only the Suzuki finding that more nuclear states correlate with fewer disputes.

Voting issue three: The Suzuki twenty fifteen evidence remains uncontested. My opponent never directly refuted it. They tried to isolate the India Pakistan case. But Suzuki measures systemic disputes across the entire international system. The resolution is a general claim about proliferation increasing stability. One incident cannot disprove a systemic pattern. The NEG's entire case rests on interpreting the Pulwama Balakot incident as proof of instability. But if nuclear weapons prevented escalation, that is stability by any reasonable definition.

Finally, the accident risk argument. My opponent says more nuclear weapons increase accident risk. I agree. But this is not a reason to reject the resolution. This is a reason to improve safety protocols. The resolution asks whether proliferation increases stability. The answer is yes, because nuclear weapons prevent major war by making it irrational. Even if accidents occur, the deterrence mechanism that prevents catastrophic conflict remains intact.

In conclusion, my opponent's strongest evidence - Pakistan's admission that nuclear deterrence de-escalated the crisis - proves the resolution. They have no evidence that proliferation decreases stability. They have no theory to explain why more nuclear states should be less stable. They have only one incident they misinterpret. The Suzuki finding stands. The deterrence mechanism works. I urge an affirmative ballot.
speeches.NR:
Judge, my opponent claims nuclear proliferation increases stability. I will prove it destroys stability by creating a world where every conventional crisis demands nuclear brinkmanship as a safety net. Let me be clear: the resolution is false under any reasonable definition of stability.

First, Pakistan’s own admission proves instability. In 2019, Pakistan stated nuclear deterrence was "required to de-escalate" the Pulwama-Balakot crisis. This isn’t proof of stability—it’s proof of crisis. If proliferation truly increased stability, why would Pakistan need nuclear deterrence to prevent a conventional conflict from escalating? The answer is simple: the region was unstable enough to require that safety net. As Pakistan’s government admitted, they needed nuclear deterrence to stop the crisis from becoming war. That’s not stability—it’s fragility. Every time a crisis erupts, nuclear weapons become the only tool to prevent escalation. That’s not peace; it’s a house of cards.

Second, the AFF’s Suzuki 2015 evidence is a statistical mirage. Suzuki claims more nuclear states mean fewer disputes. But his study only counts disputes that remain conventional—ignoring how proliferation *creates* those disputes. Since 1998, India and Pakistan have had multiple major crises: the 2001 Parliament attack, 2008 Mumbai attacks, and 2019 Pulwama-Balakot. Suzuki’s data misses this because he only measures outcomes, not the constant pressure of new crises. Proliferation didn’t reduce disputes—it made them more frequent and dangerous. The AFF’s evidence ignores reality.

Third, proliferation exponentially increases arms control complexity. As Johnson 2022 states, decision-makers face "inadvertent escalation risks" as nuclear complexity grows. The Nuclear Nonproliferation Treaty was designed for a handful of powers, not today’s fragmented reality. With more nuclear states, communication channels multiply, verification becomes impossible, and crises escalate faster. Johnson proves arms control is collapsing under this weight—not strengthening stability.

Finally, stability means no crises exist, not just that crises don’t escalate to nuclear war. The Cold War had constant crises—Cuban Missile Crisis, Berlin Wall—but we don’t call it stable. True stability requires the absence of crisis, not nuclear deterrence as a safety net. When Pakistan admits they needed nuclear deterrence to de-escalate, they’re confessing the region is unstable. The AFF’s definition is too narrow. Stability isn’t avoiding nuclear war—it’s avoiding all conflict.

In conclusion: nuclear proliferation creates instability by generating conventional crises that require nuclear deterrence as a safety net. Pakistan’s admission, Suzuki’s flawed data, and Johnson’s evidence on arms control collapse prove the resolution false. The AFF’s case rests on a dangerous misreading of stability. I urge a negative ballot.
speeches.2AR:
Thank you. This is the final speech, so let me give you three clear reasons to vote affirmative on this resolution.

First, Suzuki twenty fifteen remains uncontested. My opponent never directly refuted this evidence. They tried to isolate the India Pakistan case, but Suzuki measures the entire international system, not single dyads. The resolution is a general claim about proliferation increasing stability. One incident cannot disprove a systemic pattern. When my opponent says Suzuki only counts disputes that remain conventional, that is exactly the deterrence mechanism working. Nuclear weapons prevent escalation. That is stability.

Second, the Pulwama Balakot incident proves the resolution. My opponent says Pakistan needed nuclear deterrence to de-escalate, which proves instability. I say no. That is the deterrence mechanism functioning as intended. A conventional conflict remained conventional because both sides understood the catastrophic consequences of escalation. The only reason this crisis did not become war is because nuclear weapons created a rational incentive for restraint. My opponent is actually conceding my case.

Third, my opponent's definition of stability is unrealistic. They say stability means no crises at all. But the Cold War had crises. The Cuban Missile Crisis was a major crisis. We don't call the Cold War unstable. True stability means preventing major war. Nuclear deterrence creates this stability by making aggression irrational. The Pulwama Balakot incident proves this mechanism works. It proves that nuclear weapons prevented a larger conflict.

Here is the flow. My opponent has no evidence that proliferation decreases stability. They have no theory to explain why more nuclear states should be less stable. They have only one incident they misinterpret. Suzuki twenty fifteen shows a systemic pattern of fewer disputes with more nuclear states. The Pulwama Balakot incident is a textbook example of deterrence preventing escalation. My opponent's own evidence proves the resolution.

Stability is not the absence of all tension. Stability is the absence of major war. Nuclear proliferation increases international stability because it creates deterrence relationships that prevent catastrophic conflict. The evidence is clear. The mechanism is proven. I urge an affirmative ballot.

3. It establishes the debate framework for the NC to respond to
4. It's educational without being overwhelming

all_call_records.response:
Okay, this AFF AC is arguing that nuclear proliferation INCREASES international stability, which is a complex position that goes against common intuition. I need to generate a speech that follows their skeleton and uses the evidence provided, for a community college judge who values clear structure and real-world examples. Let me analyze what I need to do carefully, given the constraints:

- The word budget is 800 words - I need to be concise
</think>

## Reasoning

This is the first AFF constructive speech, so I need to establish clear structure, logical flow, and accessible explanations for a community college judge who values real-world examples. The speech follows the provided skeleton precisely, with four contentions that build the case: deterrence expansion, dispute reduction, MAD explanation, and an anticipated objection. 

For evidence citation, I'm using the Suzuki 15 card for the empirical dispute reduction claim and the Reisman 18 card for the MAD explanation. The evidence selection is limited, so I'm not inventing any statistics or studies - I'm grounding everything in what's provided. I'm reading citations aloud naturally with credentials where available.

The speech uses accessible language with clear signposting ("Contest one," "Contest two") to help the judge follow the argument. I'm using the 'catastrophic retaliation' framing for MAD to make the abstract concept tangible. The word budget of 800 requires tight writing - every sentence must advance the argument.

## Speech Text

Judge, my opponent will tell you that more nuclear weapons mean more risk, more accidents, more war. I am going to prove the opposite. Nuclear proliferation increases international stability because it expands deterrence relationships through mutual assured destruction. This is not theoretical speculation. This is how the world avoided nuclear war for seventy-five years. This is why no major power has attacked another with conventional forces since nineteen forty-five. Let me explain why.

Contest one: Nuclear proliferation expands deterrence relationships. Every new nuclear state immediately creates at least one new deterrence relationship with each existing nuclear power. This is the foundation of stability.

Here’s why this matters. When a country develops nuclear weapons, it doesn’t just gain a weapon. It gains psychological immunity. Existing nuclear powers must now account for that new capability. They must consider the consequences of aggression. This isn’t speculation. This is the core mechanism through which proliferation increases stability.

The NEG will likely argue that new nuclear states are less responsible, more prone to miscalculation. But that’s not how deterrence works. Deterrence isn’t about perfect leadership. It’s about the certainty of catastrophic retaliation. As Reisman writes in twenty eighteen, this system of ‘reciprocal defense must assume a world of equals.’ In other words, mutual vulnerability creates mutual caution. Every new nuclear actor forces all major powers to operate under that assumption.

Contest two: A larger number of nuclear states is empirically associated with fewer disputes. This is the counterintuitive finding that proves the resolution.

Suzuki’s twenty fifteen quantitative analysis demonstrates this directly. His research shows that ‘a larger number of nuclear states are associated with fewer disputes in the international system.’ This isn’t a minor correlation. This is a systemic pattern. More nuclear powers, less conflict.

Why does this happen? Because deterrence is not zero-sum. It is networked. When Japan relies on the United States nuclear umbrella, when India and Pakistan both possess nuclear weapons, when North Korea has developed its program, each relationship creates additional barriers to aggression. The potential costs of war multiply with each new nuclear actor.

Contest three: Let me explain the mechanism. Mutual Assured Destruction creates a stable equilibrium where no rational leader would initiate a nuclear conflict because it guarantees catastrophic retaliation.

This is not a theory about perfect peace. It is a theory about catastrophic risk. The logic is simple: if Country A attacks Country B, Country B can destroy Country A. Therefore, Country A has no incentive to attack. The fear of annihilation prevents aggression because the costs outweigh any potential gains.

This is why the Cold War did not escalate to nuclear exchange. Why no major power has invaded a NATO member. The system works because leaders understand that nuclear war has no winners. As Reisman explains, this ‘odd, counter-intuitive system of reciprocal defense must assume a world of equals.’ When all major powers understand that attack guarantees destruction, they choose caution.

Contest four: The NEG will raise the objection that more nuclear weapons increase the risk of accidents, miscalculation, or unauthorized use. I accept this risk exists. But this is not a reason to reject the resolution. It is a reason to manage that risk.

The AFF case is about systemic stability, not risk elimination. Even if accidents occur, the overall stability created by deterrence relationships remains intact. The question is not whether nuclear weapons are perfect. The question is whether they increase or decrease the likelihood of large-scale aggression between major powers.

Cohen’s twenty seventeen research confirms this distinction. His work shows that ‘experience with nuclear weapons moderates the conflict propensity’ of states. In other words, over time, nuclear powers become more cautious, not less. This is not because they are more moral. It is because they understand the consequences.

In conclusion, the resolution is simple: nuclear proliferation increases international stability. It does so by expanding deterrence relationships, creating interconnected security networks that discourage conflict, and establishing the mutual assured destruction dynamic that has prevented nuclear war for generations.

The NEG’s case rests on hypothetical accidents and miscalculations. But the AFF case rests on empirical evidence and proven theory. More nuclear states, less conflict. That is the pattern. That is the stability we must recognize. I urge an affirmative ballot.
</think>
